---
title: "Diegetic Design"
aliases: ["Diegesis", "Show the World Hide the Gears", "Diegetic UI", "Diegetic Gating"]
family: "Story, Character & Voice"
media: [interactive-fiction, live-game, tabletop, puzzle-hunt]
facets:
  effects: [immersion, fictional-coherence, present-tense-world]
  problems: [fourth-wall-break, mechanical-leakage, author-intrusion]
  components: [choice-text, gate, codex-entry, player-facing-surface]
  genre: [horror, mystery, sci-fi]
provenance: "researched"
---

*Silhouette: Keeping the interface and the machinery inside the fiction, so the player meets a world and never the gears that run it.*

A diegetic surface is one a character in the story could read and understand. A locked door is diegetic. A greyed-out menu option is not. The first belongs to the world; the second belongs to the game that runs the world. Diegetic design is the discipline of keeping everything the player touches on the first side of that line, so the fiction never has to admit there is a machine underneath it.

The instinct behind it is simple to state and hard to hold. Show the world; hide the gears. A gate is dressed as an in-world obstacle, while a choice becomes something a person in the scene would actually do. The surfaces the player reads then stay clear of the words that only the author knows: a flag name, perhaps, or the quiet "you cannot do that yet." Each of those is a small crack in the world, and the player feels the draught through it even when they cannot name what broke.

This article is about that line and how to keep work on the right side of it. The hard version of the problem lives in interactive fiction, where the player drives a machine of state and choices and the writer has to dress every moving part as something the world would contain. The same discipline reaches into the physical room and onto the boardgame table, but its sharpest demands and its most useful vocabulary were worked out where the interface and the fiction share the same screen.

## The Mechanism (How it works)

### The line: would a character understand this?

The whole discipline reduces to one test. Read any surface the player will see and ask whether a character standing inside the story would understand it. [corpus] If the text assumes knowledge the world does not contain, knowledge of chapters or flags or that this is a game at all, then it is not diegetic, and it has leaked the machinery into view.

The term itself is older than games. *Diegesis* is the storyteller's word for the world the story takes place in, as against everything outside it. The film theorists borrowed it in the mid-twentieth century, and the narratologist Gérard Genette fixed the modern split between the diegetic (inside the story world) and the non-diegetic (outside it) in his work on narrative. [researched] Film sound carried the distinction into common use: a song the characters can hear on a radio is diegetic, the orchestral score swelling over the scene is not. [researched] Games inherited both the word and the line, and then had to decide, surface by surface, which side each element sits on.

That decision is not always a clean two-way split. The clearest map of the options comes from a study of first-person shooter interfaces by Erik Fagerholt and Magnus Lorentzon, who sorted every interface element along two questions. Does it belong to the story's fiction? And does it sit in the world's space or flat on the screen? [researched] The four boxes that fall out of those questions are worth carrying around, because they name the choices a designer actually faces.

| Type | In the fiction? | In the world's space? | What it looks like |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diegetic | Yes | Yes | A hologram the character reads; a note pinned to a wall [researched] |
| Non-diegetic | No | No | A health bar or ammo counter laid over the screen [researched] |
| Spatial | No | Yes | A floating waypoint marker the character cannot see [researched] |
| Meta | Yes | No | Blood on the camera lens standing in for the character's injury [researched] |

Most game interfaces mix all four, and there is no rule that says everything must be diegetic. A health bar is read in a glance and costs the player nothing to parse, where a wholly in-world health cue can cost attention the moment demands elsewhere. The point of the map is not to forbid the non-diegetic. It is to make every placement a choice rather than a default, so the elements that break the fiction do so because the designer decided they earn their keep.

### Diegetic gates: a locked door, not a greyed-out option

The gate is where the line bites hardest, because a gate has to communicate a *rule* and rules are exactly the machinery the fiction is meant to hide. The non-diegetic gate says the quiet part aloud: this option is locked until you obtain the foreman's seal codeword. [corpus] The diegetic gate says the same thing as the world would say it. The foreman's seal is required to enter the restricted level. The guards demand proof of membership and turn you away without it. [corpus] The state underneath is identical. Only the surface changes, and the surface is the whole game.

The craft of a diegetic gate is to name the obstacle as something the world contains and to imply the way through it without naming a quest. [corpus] What blocks the door: guards, a lock, a missing word. What the world calls the thing you need, whether a seal or a token. Where someone might go to find it. The non-diegetic version of each of those leaks a different piece of the machine. "Requires: union_member flag" leaks the schema. "Complete quest A first" leaks the structure. "Insufficient progress" leaks the existence of a progress counter the character has no way to know about. The fix in every case is to ask what the obstacle amounts to in the world and to let the world describe it.

The same line runs straight through the physical room, where it is drawn in hardware instead of prose. A combination padlock on a drawer is a diegetic gate. The lock is a real object in the fiction, and a character could stand in front of it and understand exactly what it wants. [synthesis] The message "you don't have the key yet" is not diegetic, and a well-built room never needs to say it, because the lock says it already. [corpus] This is the same surface-hygiene question the locked door poses in interactive fiction, and it is why the gate and the lock are two faces of one idea: the boundary made physical is [Lock Taxonomy](../space-props-materiality/lock-taxonomy.qmd), and the rule it has to enforce while still reading as a gate is the fairness contract of [Cluing & Fairness](../puzzles-clues-information/cluing-and-fairness.qmd). A gate must look like a gate; a diegetic gate must also look like a thing the world would contain.

### Choices as actions, not menu items

In interactive fiction the choice is the most-read surface of all, and it is easy to let the menu register creep in. A choice that says "click here to continue" or "proceed to the next scene" admits there is a scene structure and a next thing. [corpus] A choice that says "choose wisely" admits the whole frame: this is a game, and you are being scored. [corpus] The diegetic choice is something a person in the scene would actually do, written as an action with a verb and a target. Confront the foreman. Search his office after hours. Report to the steward. [corpus]

There is a second trap that is quieter than the meta one. A choice that tells the player its own outcome ("this will anger the foreman") hands away the suspense and stops being a choice the character makes blind, which is what a real decision is. [corpus] The player should be able to *predict* that different actions lead to different consequences without being told what they are. When two options read as near-synonyms, "investigate further" against "keep looking into it," they fail a different way: they are diegetic enough but they are not really two choices, because the world cannot tell them apart. The construction of distinct, contrastive options is the choice-architecture craft of [Branching Narrative Craft](../structure-pacing/branching-narrative-craft.qmd); diegetic design only asks that each option, once distinct, also reads as an act in the world rather than a line on a control panel.

### Surface hygiene: keeping the gears out of sight

Past the gates and the choices comes a broader and duller discipline. The machinery has to stay out of every surface the player can read. The internal names a project runs on, whether a flag identifier or an anchor label, never belong in player-facing prose. The failure mode of letting one slip is mechanical leakage. [corpus] A codeword printed where a character's name should be is the most literal possible break in the world, and the defence against it is partly automated (you can grep for the patterns that should never appear) and partly a human read of anything subtler. [corpus]

Author intrusion is the same break wearing a more respectable coat. A codex entry that explains the guild is "a major faction in Chapter 2" whose allies "get access to the restricted docks" has stopped being a document from inside the world and become a strategy note about the game. [corpus] The repair is to write reference material as an in-world scholar would. The scholar records public knowledge and the history that is not secret, with no mention of the player or the chapter or what becomes important later. [corpus] What is commonly known, the scholar can say. What a character secretly plans, or who the masked figure really is, the scholar cannot, because none of that is knowledge the world has yet released. So diegetic discipline doubles as spoiler hygiene: the test "would an in-world observer know this?" catches the future-tense leak and the foreshadowing leak in the same pass.

Uncertainty is the case where surface hygiene meets honest sourcing, and it has a clean solution. When a researched fact is solid, state it plainly. When it is shaky, phrase it so the fiction carries the doubt instead of a citation: "it was said that," "rumour held," "no one knew for certain." [corpus] A citation on a player-facing surface is itself a leak, a footnote the world cannot contain, so the diegetic move is to let the prose hold the uncertainty in a voice the world would actually use. Which leak counts as a voice belonging to the fiction's surface against one sitting outside it is the same question [Character Voice](character-voice.qmd) asks of every speaker; here the speaker is the narration itself.

::: {.callout-note}
### The Specimen
*Dead Space* (2008) pushed diegetic interface design about as far as a game can. The protagonist's health is shown by a glowing strip running up the spine of his suit, read by looking at his back rather than at a corner of the screen. Ammunition appears as a small hologram projected from the gun only when he raises it. The inventory and the map and the stored audio logs all open as holographic panels the character is shown looking at, standing in the world while the action keeps running around them.
<!-- vale ai-tells.FormalRegister = NO -->
The lead interface designer's framing at GDC was that the team was "not just diegetic by design, we were diegetic by implementation," meaning the choice was carried all the way down into how each element was built rather than bolted on as a coat of paint. [researched]
<!-- vale ai-tells.FormalRegister = YES -->
The payoff is exactly the immersion the discipline promises. With no overlay anywhere on the screen, the player must read their own danger off the world itself. That reading deepens the vulnerability the horror is built on. The cost is real too. A glance at a flat number beats turning to read a character's back. The design accepted that friction on purpose. [synthesis]
:::

### When the frame itself is fiction

There is a far end of this discipline where the diegesis swallows the whole apparatus, including the question of whether you are playing at all. The alternate-reality game and the self-concealing puzzle hunt build a fiction that refuses to announce itself as a fiction. Its artefacts are posted into the real world and its recruitment reads as real, with no visible seam between the game and the world it hides in. [synthesis] The Cicada 3301 puzzles are the standing example, a trail of images and coordinates and cryptography that never once said "this is a game," so that every solver had to hold the question "is this real?" as part of the experience. [corpus] That is diegetic design carried to its limit. The frame, the thing every other medium keeps non-diegetic so the player knows where the exit is, has itself been pulled inside the story. The trade is severe and not always wise, because a player who cannot find the frame cannot always find their way to consent or to the door, but it shows the full reach of the idea: anything can be made diegetic, up to and including the fact of the game.

### The boundary: three homes for one idea

Diegesis is one concept with three workshops, and it helps to keep the boundaries clear so the right craft is reached for. This article is the interactive-fiction and narrative-interface home, where the work is the surface the reader reads. A gate is written as prose and a choice as an act, while the codex is kept in an in-world voice throughout. The live and physical version, where the world is a built room and immersion is carried by what the senses meet rather than what the page says, is [Theme, Immersion & Sensory Environment](../space-props-materiality/theme-immersion-and-sensory-environment.qmd); a diegetic device in that room, a screen or prop that behaves as a real object in the fiction, is [Digital Props](../space-props-materiality/digital-props.qmd). The boardgame version asks a different question again: not whether the surface hides the machine but whether the theme and the rules say the same thing, the integrity problem that fails as the "reskin," where a theme is paint over mechanics that argue something else entirely. That is [Theme-Mechanism Integration](../systems-mechanics/theme-mechanism-integration.qmd), and the deeper claim that a mechanic can itself carry meaning rather than merely hide is [IF Mechanics & Procedural Rhetoric](../systems-mechanics/if-mechanics-and-procedural-rhetoric.qmd). Diegetic design hides the gears; procedural rhetoric makes the gears themselves say something. They are neighbours, and worth not confusing.

::: {.callout-warning}
### The Hazard
The seductive failure is to treat "diegetic" as a quality score, where more is always better and every non-diegetic element is a flaw to be hunted down. It is not. A wholly diegetic interface can tax the player past the point where the fiction was worth it: a health cue you have to stop and read costs attention in the exact moments the game most wants you watching the world, and a gate so deeply in-world that its requirement is never legible leaves the player stuck without knowing why. The tell is when the discipline starts fighting clarity. A playtester cannot find their health, or cannot tell what a gate wants, or cannot locate the edge of the game they have agreed to play. Diegesis serves immersion; immersion serves the experience. The moment the line is being held for its own sake against the player's ability to understand what is happening, the score has become the goal and the goal has been lost. [synthesis]
:::

## Where this fits

**The bigger picture.** Diegetic design is the surface contract of the whole Story family. Where [Character Voice](character-voice.qmd) governs whether a speaker sounds like a person and [Exposition & Subtext](exposition-and-subtext.qmd) governs how much the world withholds, diegetic design governs whether any of it reads as belonging to the world at all, or whether the machinery shows through. It sits underneath the others the way fairness sits under puzzles. A violation here breaks the whole frame, no matter how good the voice or the plot above it. In interactive fiction it carries a load it never carries in static prose, because a real machine sits underneath. That apparatus of state and gates and branching choices has every part dressed as something the world would contain. The setting that the surface presents, and the way a place can be made to feel discovered rather than fabricated, are [Environmental Storytelling & Setting as Character](environmental-storytelling-and-setting-as-character.qmd) and [Worldbuilding Patterns](worldbuilding-patterns.qmd); diegetic design is the rule that none of that world admits the game running it.

**Next steps.** Once the surface is clean, the next concern is usually the gates and choices it dresses: the choice architecture beneath the diegetic skin is [Branching Narrative Craft](../structure-pacing/branching-narrative-craft.qmd), and the fairness a gate must keep while still reading as an obstacle is [Cluing & Fairness](../puzzles-clues-information/cluing-and-fairness.qmd). To carry the same discipline into a physical room, where immersion is built from sensory layers rather than prose, work through [Theme, Immersion & Sensory Environment](../space-props-materiality/theme-immersion-and-sensory-environment.qmd) and the diegetic objects of [Digital Props](../space-props-materiality/digital-props.qmd). And to cross from hiding the machine to making the machine mean something, the step is [IF Mechanics & Procedural Rhetoric](../systems-mechanics/if-mechanics-and-procedural-rhetoric.qmd).

## Sources

- Genette, Gérard. *Narrative Discourse: An Essay in Method*. Translated by Jane E. Lewin. Cornell University Press, 1980. (The diegetic / non-diegetic distinction in narratology.)
- Winters, Ben. "The Non-Diegetic Fallacy: Film, Music, and Narrative Space." *Music & Letters* 91, no. 2 (2010): 224–244. <https://oro.open.ac.uk/29647/>
- Fagerholt, Erik, and Magnus Lorentzon. *Beyond the HUD: User Interfaces for Increased Player Immersion in FPS Games*. Master's thesis, Chalmers University of Technology, 2009. <https://www.researchgate.net/publication/277202228_Beyond_the_HUD_-_User_Interfaces_for_Increased_Player_Immersion_in_FPS_Games>
<!-- vale ai-tells.FormalRegister = NO -->
- Ignacio, Dino. "Crafting Destruction: The Evolution of the Dead Space User Interface." Game Developers Conference, 2013. (The "diegetic by implementation" framing.) Overview: <https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/game-ui-discoveries-what-players-want>
<!-- vale ai-tells.FormalRegister = YES -->
- *Dead Space*. Electronic Arts / Visceral Games, 2008. (Spine health strip, holographic inventory and ammunition as diegetic interface.)
- Bell, Joel ("gamer_152"). "Markers: A Deep Dive into Dead Space's UI." Giant Bomb, 2016. <https://www.giantbomb.com/profile/gamer_152/blog/markers-i-a-deep-dive-into-dead-spaces-ui/249377/>
- "Cicada 3301." Wikipedia. (The self-concealing puzzle trail that never declared itself a game.) <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cicada_3301>
